content:
# 2 Peter Chapter 3 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), pages 270-271 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/2PE_003_page_001.jpg, 2PE_003_page_002.jpg

**1** Dear friends, this is now the second letter I am writing to you. In both of them I have been trying to stir up your honest thinking by reminding you of these things, **2** so that you will remember the words spoken long ago by the holy prophets and the command of the Lord and Savior given through your apostles.

**3** First and foremost, understand this: in the last days mockers will come, living by their own desires and ridiculing the truth. **4** They will say, "So where is this 'coming' he promised? Ever since our ancestors died, everything has gone on exactly as it has from the beginning of creation." **5** But they deliberately ignore this fact: long ago, by God's word, the heavens came into existence and the earth was formed out of water and through water, **6** and it was by those same means that the world of that time was deluged with water and destroyed. **7** But by that same word, the present heavens and earth are being reserved for fire, kept for the day of judgment and the destruction of ungodly people.

**8** But do not let this one thing escape your notice, dear friends: with the Lord, one day is like a thousand years, and a thousand years are like a single day. **9** The Lord is not slow to fulfill his promise, as some people understand slowness. Instead, he is being patient with you, not wanting anyone to be destroyed, but wanting everyone to come to a change of heart.

**10** But the day of the Lord will arrive like a thief. On that day the heavens will disappear with a tremendous roar, the elements will be consumed by intense heat and dissolved, and the earth and everything done on it will be laid bare. **11** Since everything is going to be dissolved in this way, what kind of people should you be? You should live holy and godly lives, **12** eagerly waiting for and hastening the arrival of the day of God. On that day the heavens will be set ablaze and dissolved, and the elements will melt in the intense heat. **13** But in keeping with his promise, we are looking forward to new heavens and a new earth, where righteousness is at home.

**14** So then, dear friends, since you are looking forward to these things, make every effort to be found at peace with God — spotless and without fault. **15** And regard the patience of our Lord as an opportunity for salvation, just as our dear brother Paul also wrote to you with the wisdom God gave him. **16** He speaks about these matters in all his letters. Some things in them are hard to understand, which ignorant and unstable people twist and distort, as they do with the rest of the Scriptures, to their own ruin.

**17** Therefore, dear friends, since you already know about these things, be on your guard so that you are not carried away by the deception of lawless people and lose your own firm footing. **18** Instead, keep growing in the grace and knowledge of our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ. To him be glory both now and to the day of eternity. Amen.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 1 — "stir up your honest thinking":** The Greek *diegeiro humon en hupomneesei teen eilikrinee dianoian* (I arouse your sincere mind by way of reminder) uses *eilikrinee* (pure, sincere, unmixed) to describe the kind of thinking Peter wants to activate. The dynamic rendering "honest thinking" captures the sense of a mind uncontaminated by the false teaching addressed in chapter 2.

**Verse 4 — "So where is this 'coming' he promised?":** The Greek *pou estin hee epangelia tees parousias autou* (where is the promise of his coming?) is rendered as direct speech to convey the mocking tone. The scoffers treat the delay of Christ's return as evidence against its reality. The word *parousia* (arrival, presence) was the standard term for a royal visit — the mockers are questioning whether the King is ever actually coming.

**Verse 5 — "the earth was formed out of water and through water":** The Greek *gee ex hudatos kai di hudatos sunesteosa* (earth consisting out of water and through water) reflects the Genesis creation narrative where land emerges from the primordial waters. The Sinaiticus reading here is consistent with the broader tradition. Peter's point is that the same divine word that created can also destroy.

**Verse 7 — "reserved for fire":** The Greek *puri teereoumenoi* (being kept for fire) presents a stark parallel with verse 5-6. Water destroyed the ancient world; fire is reserved for the present one. The Sinaiticus text reads the present heavens and earth as *tetheraurismenoi* (stored up / treasured), an ironic use — they are "treasured" for destruction.

**Verse 8 — "one day is like a thousand years":** Peter draws on Psalm 90:4 (LXX 89:4). The point is not a mathematical formula for calculating dates but a radical statement about divine perspective: God's relationship to time is entirely different from ours. What feels like unbearable delay to us is nothing to God.

**Verse 9 — "not wanting anyone to be destroyed, but wanting everyone to come to a change of heart":** The Greek *mee boulomenos tinas apolesthai alla pantas eis metanoian chooreesai* (not wishing any to perish but all to come to repentance) reveals the theological reason behind what looks like delay. God's patience is not inaction — it is purposeful waiting driven by mercy. The Sinaiticus text reads *eis humas* (toward you) rather than *eis heemas* (toward us), directing this patience specifically at the letter's recipients.

**Verse 10 — "will be laid bare":** The Sinaiticus reads *heurethesetai* (will be found / will be laid bare) rather than the later variant *katakaesetai* (will be burned up). This is a significant textual variant. The Sinaiticus reading suggests that on judgment day the earth and its works will be exposed and discovered — nothing will remain hidden. The rendering "laid bare" captures this sense of uncovering rather than incineration.

**Verse 12 — "hastening the arrival":** The Greek *speudontas teen parousian* (hastening the coming) is a striking idea — that human conduct can somehow accelerate the divine timetable. Peter seems to suggest that holy living and repentance (verse 9) play a role in bringing about the consummation. The dynamic rendering preserves this provocative concept.

**Verse 13 — "where righteousness is at home":** The Greek *en hois dikaiosunee katoikei* (in which righteousness dwells) uses *katoikei* (to settle down, to make one's home) rather than a word for temporary residence. Righteousness will not be a visitor in the new creation — it will live there permanently. "Where righteousness is at home" conveys this settled, permanent quality.

**Verse 15-16 — Paul's letters as Scripture:** Peter's reference to Paul's letters is remarkable: he treats them as comparable to *hai loipai graphai* (the rest of the Scriptures), placing Paul's writings alongside the Hebrew Bible as authoritative sacred text. This is one of the earliest witnesses to the canonical status of Pauline letters. The acknowledgment that Paul's writings contain things *dusnoeta* (hard to understand) is a refreshingly honest assessment from a fellow apostle.

**Verse 17 — "lose your own firm footing":** The Greek *tou idiou steerigmou ekpesete* (fall from your own stability) uses *steerigmos* (firmness, steadfastness), related to the verb *steerizein* (to make firm) — the very word Jesus used when he told Peter to "strengthen" his brothers (Luke 22:32). The rendering "lose your own firm footing" captures both the danger and the physical metaphor.

**Verse 18 — "to the day of eternity":** The Greek *eis hemeran aionos* (unto the day of eternity) is an unusual phrase found only here in the New Testament. It merges the concept of a specific day with the concept of unending time — a single day that never ends. The Sinaiticus text concludes with *ameen* (amen), sealing the letter with a liturgical affirmation.

**Sinaiticus-specific note:** The text of 2 Peter 3 begins at the bottom of page 270 (column 4) and continues across page 271 in columns 1-3. The book concludes in column 3 before 1 John begins in column 4. The most significant Sinaiticus variant in this chapter is *heurethesetai* (will be found/laid bare) in verse 10, where later manuscripts read *katakaesetai* (will be burned up). This earlier reading, supported by the manuscript's 4th-century date, is widely regarded by modern textual critics as closer to the original. The Sinaiticus text also reads *eis humas* (toward you) in verse 9, consistent with the direct, pastoral address of the letter.
